docs.title: Alexander Skog
docs.text: I use this site as a working notebook for infrastructure, OpenShift, Kubernetes, virtualization, and the practical things I learn while building and operating systems.

docs.title: What you'll find here
docs.text:
• Hands-on guides for OpenShift and Kubernetes
  • Lab notes from infrastructure experiments
  • Short write-ups on networking, platform work, and operations

docs.title: Current focus
docs.text: Right now the site is strongest around OpenShift. A good next step is to grow it into a broader home for platform engineering notes, with a few recurring themes instead of scattered one-off pages.

docs.title: 1. Core pages
docs.text:
• Home: a short introduction and featured posts
  • About: who you are, what you work on, and what this site covers
  • Contact: GitHub, LinkedIn, email, and resume if you want

docs.title: 2. Topic areas
docs.text:
• OpenShift and Kubernetes
  • Networking
  • Virtualization and homelab
  • Automation and tooling

docs.title: 3. Post types
docs.text:
• How-to guides
  • Build logs
  • Troubleshooting notes
  • Opinionated reference pages

docs.title: Featured posts
docs.text:
• Manage users with htpasswd
  • Running Squid as a container on OpenShift 4
  • Single-node OpenShift on OpenShift Virtualization

docs.title: Good content ideas for this site
OpenShift and Kubernetes
docs.text:
• How you structure a home lab or test cluster
  • Day-2 operations notes that save time later
  • Common install mistakes and how to recover from them
  • Bare metal versus virtualized OpenShift tradeoffs

docs.title: Networking
docs.text:
• DNS, ingress, load balancers, and firewall setup for lab environments
  • Proxy patterns that actually worked for you
  • Small diagrams explaining traffic flow in your setups

docs.title: Virtualization
docs.text:
• What worked well with OpenShift Virtualization
  • Resource sizing lessons from real deployments
  • When to use VMs versus containers in your environment

docs.title: Personal angle
docs.text:
• What you are learning this quarter
  • Why you built your lab the way you did
  • A "Now" section with your current experiments

docs.title: A simple publishing rhythm
docs.text:
If you want this site to grow steadily, aim for short posts with a repeatable pattern:

  1. Problem
  2. Context
  3. Steps
  4. Validation
  5. Lessons learned

That format fits your current technical topics really well and keeps the site practical.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
I'm Alexander, and this site is where I collect practical notes from working with platform technologies.

My strongest topics right now are:

  • OpenShift
  • Kubernetes
  • Virtualization
  • Infrastructure troubleshooting
  • Networking in lab and platform environments

docs.title: What I want this site to be
docs.text:
Not just a generic blog, but a useful reference for real platform work:

  • concise enough to scan quickly
  • detailed enough to repeat the steps later
  • honest about what worked, what failed, and what I would change next time

docs.title: Suggested additions
docs.text:
This page will become much stronger if you add:

  • a short professional bio
  • your current role or focus area
  • a link to your GitHub profile
  • a link to LinkedIn or another contact method
  • a short note about your lab environment or interests

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: The easiest way to turn this into a real personal site is to make it easy to reach you.

docs.title: Add here
docs.text:
• GitHub: https://github.com/askog96
  • LinkedIn: your profile link
  • Email: a public contact address if you want one
  • Resume or CV: optional downloadable link

docs.title: Tip
docs.text: If you do not want to publish your email directly, link to GitHub and LinkedIn and keep this page simple.

docs.text: Here are content ideas that fit the direction of the site and the posts you already have.

docs.title: Good post template
docs.text: Use this structure for future posts:

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: What the post helps the reader do.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text: Versions, topology, and assumptions.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: The commands or configuration.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: How you know it worked.

docs.title: Lessons learned
docs.text: What you would repeat or do differently next time.

docs.title: Create and Configure new users using htpassd
docs.text:
Create a new user new_admin with the password redhat in the existing file ~/auth-providers/htpasswd:

  htpasswd -c -B -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd new_admin redhat

-c: Create a new file. If the file already exists, it will be overwritten. -B: Use bcrypt encryption for the password, which is more secure. -b: Use the password from the command line rather than prompting for it. ~/auth-providers/htpasswd: The path to the file where the user credentials will be stored. new_admin: The username of the new user. redhat: The password for the new user.

⚠ Caution: Running this command with the `-c` option will overwrite the existing htpasswd file. Make sure you do not lose existing user data.

docs.title: Create new secret used by OAuth
docs.text: oc create secret generic localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

docs.title: Rollout deployment to apply configuration
docs.text: oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication

docs.title: Add cluster-admin to user
docs.text: oc adm policy add-cluster-role-to-user cluster-admin new_admin

docs.title: Add a user to existing OAuth config
Extract the file data from secret
docs.text: oc extract secret/localusers -n openshift-config --to ~/auth-providers/ --confirm

docs.title: Add new user to htpasswd called manager with password redhat
docs.text: htpasswd -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd manager redhat

docs.title: Update secret used by OAuth
docs.text: oc set data secret/localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

docs.title: Rollout deployment to apply configuration
docs.text: oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication

docs.title: Running squidproxy as a container on OpenShift 4
docs.text:
After I have been running squidproxy on a physical machine, I decided to give it a try to run it as a container.

  htpasswd -c -B -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd new_admin redhat
⚠ Caution: Running this command with the `-c` option will overwrite the existing htpasswd file. Make sure you do not lose existing user data.

docs.title: Create new secret used by OAuth
docs.text: oc create secret generic localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

docs.title: Rollout deployment to apply configuration
docs.text: oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication

docs.title: Add cluster-admin to user

Extract the file data from secret

docs.title: Single node OpenShift running on OpenShift Virtualization
docs.text: A quite large project to get Single node OpenShift to run inside a VM on OpenShift Virtualization. The intention is to more effectivly using the hardware but also enabling an aaS concept with Single node OpenShift

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before proceeding, its recommened to use the following: - ## Hardware: Ensure that your system is running one of the following: - Any server with atleast 192GB of memory. - Intel Xenon 6132 CPU - Intel xxv710 or Intel E810 NIC

  • Admin rights to install required software

  • Access to the project repository

  • Stable internet connection

  • Access to required internal/external resources

  • Docker (if containerization is used)
  • Virtual environment (recommended for Python projects)
  htpasswd -c -B -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd new_admin redhat
⚠ Caution: Running this command with the `-c` option will overwrite the existing htpasswd file. Make sure you do not lose existing user data.

docs.title: Operating System:
docs.text:
• Red Hat OpenShift 4.15 or later.
  • OpenShift Virtualization x.zz
  • List of operators TBA
